EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level shortwave with a modified arctic cold front brought moderate to heavy snow showers across the Cascades, Blues, and Wallowas, with moderate to heavy snow being observed along the Blue Mountain foothills, and in valley locations across the northeast Oregon mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ski Bluewood ski resort measured 16 inches of new snow over a 24 hour period ending at 0451 PST on Dec. 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level weather system pushed a cold front into the Pacific Northwest late Dec. 18th, where the frontal boundary had stalled across southeast Washington and northeast Oregon through Dec. 20th where the stationary frontal boundary had weakened in the afternoon. As a result, bands of rain, freezing rain, and snow were produced across portions of the forecast area during the 19th and 20th, with snow being reported across the lower elevations in the Columbia Basin and mountain locations across southeast Washington and northeast Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 9 miles NNE of City of The Dalles, OR measured 14 inches of new snow over a 24 hour period ending at 1010 PST on Dec. 20th. A public report 6 miles NNW of Goldendale, WA measured an estimated 8 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending 0714 PST on Dec. 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level weather system pushed a cold front into the Pacific Northwest late Dec. 18th, where the frontal boundary had stalled across southeast Washington and northeast Oregon through Dec. 20th where the stationary frontal boundary had weakened in the afternoon. As a result, bands of rain, freezing rain, and snow were produced across portions of the forecast area during the 19th and 20th, with snow being reported across the lower elevations in the Columbia Basin and mountain locations across southeast Washington and northeast Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report 1 mile NW of Lyle, WA measured 6 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 1405 PST on Dec. 20th. A trained spotter in White Salmon, WA measured 4 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period ending at 1044 PST on Dec. 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and cold front passage at the surface produced snow showers across the Cascade and Blue mountain ranges in Oregon and Washington, while a low level jet associated with this winter storm mixed down strong gusts across the Washington Columbia Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gable Mountain station belonging to the Hanford Meteorological Monitoring Network (HMMN) 22 miles NNW of Richland, WA measured a peak wind gust of 78 mph at 715 PST. The EOC (HMMN) station 13 miles NW of Richland, WA measured a peak wind gust of 70 mph at 845 PST. 7 miles NNW of Richland, WA, social media reports showed an overturned truck and a travel trailer along State Route 240 in the Hanford area. Local Law enforcement also reported one truck driver was hospitalized due to the accident. Also, 2 miles SE of Kennewick, WA a large tree with a 40 inch circumference base was uprooted by the strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough and cold front passage at the surface produced snow showers across the Cascade and Blue mountain ranges in Oregon and Washington, while a low level jet associated with this winter storm mixed down strong gusts across the Washington Columbia Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ski Bluewood measured 10 inches of new snow in a 24 hour period, and 16 inches of new snow over a 48 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another series of weather systems moved across Western Washington January 4-5, 2021, bringing lowland rain, mountain snow, and gusty winds to the area. Breezy to locally windy conditions were forecast on January 5, especially along the Coast and over the North Interior, including areas of Western Whatcom and Skagit Counties, the Admiralty Inlet area, and the San Juan Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind forecast for the Admiralty Inlet area led to the cancellation of all ferry sailing to Port Townsend and Coupeville during the morning hours of the 5th. Mesonet stations in Jefferson and Island counties reported wind gusts between 52-57 mph from mid-morning through early afternoon.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow affected the Northern Southern Cascades January 5th and 6th as a series of storm systems moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began falling during the early morning hours of January 5, increasing in intensity through the evening and overnight hours of the 6th. In a 24 hour period, NWAC snowfall data indicated a little over 20 inches of new snow near the Mount Baker Ski Resort. Snow then quickly tapered through the rest of the morning on the 6th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow affected the Northern Southern Cascades January 5th and 6th as a series of storm systems moved across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began during the morning hours of the 5th, peaking in intensity during the evening of the 5th and overnight into the 6th. According to NWAC snow data, from arounf 6PM PST on the 5th through 6AM PST on the 6th, around 12 inches of new fell around Paradise at Mount Rainier National Park. Light snow then continued through the remainder of the morning before tapering.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems brought more lowland rain, gusty winds, and heavy mountain snow to the area towards the middle of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began during the evening hours of January 11th and quickly increased in coverage overnight and through the morning hours of the 12th. Heavy snow then continued through the night of the 12th into the early morning hours of the 13th. According to NWAC snow data, from around 6AM PST on the 12th through 6AM PST on the 13th, around 22 inches of new snow fell around the Mount Baker Ski Resort.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems brought more lowland rain, gusty winds, and heavy mountain snow to the area towards the middle of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling during the morning hours on the 11th and continued through the 12th. In a 12 hour period, spanning from roughly 5PM PST on the 11th through 5AM PST on the 12th, around 14 inches of new snow was recorded at Snoqualmie Pass. Overall, throughout the entirety of this event from around 11 AM PST on the 11th through 6AM on the 12th around 19 inches of new snow fell around Snoqualmie Pass.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of mountain snow affected the Northern Cascades and the Olympics at the end of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began just after midnight on the 30th and continued through the 31st. 24 hour snow amounts beginning around 6AM PST on the 30th through 6AM PST on the 31st around the Mount Baker Ski Resort were around 20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another series of weather systems moved across Western Washington January 4-5, 2021, bringing lowland rain, mountain snow, and gusty winds to the area. Breezy to locally windy conditions were forecast on January 5, especially along the Coast and over the North Interior, including areas of Western Whatcom and Skagit Counties, the Admiralty Inlet area, and the San Juan Islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the morning and early afternoon hours, strong winds plagued the San Juan Islands, with Mesonet DW7371 reporting a wind gust of 60 mph on Lopez Island. Mesonet DW5375 on Decatur Island reported a wind gust of 59 mph. Trees on power lines also caused power outages on Orcas Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems affected Western Washington around the middle of January, with dangerous beach conditions expected to set up by January 12. This combined with King Tides led to the potential for coastal flooding through the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal flooding inundated a picnic area at Rialto Beach in Olympic National Park during the morning hours of January 13.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems affected Western Washington around the middle of January, with dangerous beach conditions expected to set up by January 12. This combined with King Tides led to the potential for coastal flooding through the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal Flooding from King Tides led to flooding of the Bridge Community Center in Birch Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper trough off the Pacific Northwest Coast pushed a surface frontal system across the Pendleton forecast area starting on the evening of Nov. 8th, and departing in the afternoon of the next day. The strongest winds were associated with the warm front passage after 9 pm on the 8th, and persisted into the early morning hours on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station AS070 4 miles ESE of Walla Walla, WA reported a peak wind gust of 65 mph at 2342 PST on Nov. 8th. The KALW ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph at 0320 PST on Nov. 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, a strong cold front passage across the Pendleton forecast area produced strong wind gusts across lower elevation areas, causing reports of damage in north central Oregon and in the Lower Columbia Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS K1YT at the Vagabond Army Airfield in WA measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph at 1242 PST. The ASOS KYKM measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph at 1237 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was a marginal but long-lived heavy snow event that eventually produced 10-20 of snow in the Cascades.  There were two bursts of snow, each for 6-12, very consistent across the Cascades of southern Washington down to Lane County Oregon. Gusts 50-70 mph were common in the Cascades, with the ODOT sensor at Santiam Pass even reporting a gust to 52 mph.  Ski lifts were closed due to gusts 65-85 mph.  One of the higher ski lifts of Mt Hood Meadows reported a gust to 89 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow produced 10-20 of snow in the Cascades.  There were two bursts of snow, each for 6-12, very consistent across the Cascades of southern Washington.  Gusts 50-70 mph were common in the Cascades.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact area of low pressure passed over the northwest corner of the Olympic Peninsula during the evening hours of January 12 into the early morning hours of Wednesday January 13, producing a very strong pressure gradient as pressure quickly began to rise on its backside. This led to a short period of strong winds, some of which on the backside of the low was channeled and enhanced by topography.||By daybreak on the 13th, as the wind began to subside, over 550,000 power outages were reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around the Seattle metro reported wind gusts ranging from 40-60 mph beginning during the late evening hours and through the early morning hours of the 13th. SeaTac reported a wind gust of 58 mph, with Federal Way reporting 61 mph, both shortly after midnight. The Seattle Tacoma Airport reported a wind gust of 49 mph, with other locations reporting wind gusts of 52 mph (Bellevue), 46 mph (Renton Airport), 54 mph (Des Moines).||Multiple roads in Seattle were closed due to downed powerlines during the early morning hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around Tacoma reported wind gusts of 40-60 mph beginning during the late evening hours on the 12th and continuing through the early morning hours of the 13th. Ft Lewis and JBLM reported gusts of 58 mph, with other reports of 56 mph (Tacoma), 58 mph (Greenwater).||In South Tacoma, a tree came down on a home and demolished it, trapping one of the occupants (occupant was rescued and unharmed).||The North Meridian Bridge closed in both directions for a period of time due to a tree hitting the bridge and causing damage to the bridge.

Sandy Pt Shores reported a wind gust of 72 mph and Ferndale reported a gust of 70 mph. Other reports included: 65 mph (Birch Bay), 52 mph (Lynden), 49 mph (Bellingham), 45 mph (Chuckanut Village).

Other wind gust reports around the county ranged from 40 mph to 55 mph. Owl Mountain reported a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several weather systems moved across Western Washington at the beginning of the new year, bringing with them widespread rain and gusty winds to the area. Several observations sites reported gusts in excess of 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the morning hours of January 2, Mesonet EW2796 reported a wind gust of 58 mph 9 miles northwest of Langley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A spring storm brought accumulating snowfall to the Washington Cascades beginning on April 7th and continuing into the 10th as snow levels dropped to between 1000-2000 feet. Snowfall was heaviest between the 7th-8th and mostly affected the North Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall in the North Cascades during the mid-morning hours of April 7th. Snow accumulated quickly as snow levels dropped to 2000 feet or below. The Mount Baker Ski area recorded around 13.5 inches of new snow in a 12 hour period between 9am and 9pm. Snow continued into the overnight period, gradually tapering into the morning hours of the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough drove an arctic airmass into southwest Washington. As multiple Pacific fronts moved over the area, a major elevation winter storm with heavy snow and freezing rain occurred over a multi-day period. This was a major, widespread, multi-faceted winter storm that caused major problems across the forecast area, even (and especially) in the lowlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow fell over the region beginning the night of the 11th and continued in waves through the 14th. Snow accumulations of 8 to 14 inches were fairly consistently reported across the zone. Widespread transportation delays occurred including along I-5. Scattered power outages were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough drove an arctic airmass into southwest Washington. As multiple Pacific fronts moved over the area, a major elevation winter storm with heavy snow and freezing rain occurred over a multi-day period. This was a major, widespread, multi-faceted winter storm that caused major problems across the forecast area, even (and especially) in the lowlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overall, ice amounts were a little less than the Oregon side of the metro area.  Totals ranged from 0.25 to 0.75, and many thousands lost power in Clark County. ||One ice-related fatality:  At 1715 PST on the 14th, one adult male, aged 57, died when he was heading southbound and lost control of his vehicle on the icy road surface, driving off the side of the Glenn Jackson Bridge near Government Island.  The vehicle and the driver's body were later found on February 17.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough drove an arctic airmass into southwest Washington. As multiple Pacific fronts moved over the area, a major elevation winter storm with heavy snow and freezing rain occurred over a multi-day period. This was a major, widespread, multi-faceted winter storm that caused major problems across the forecast area, even (and especially) in the lowlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought several rounds of heavy snow and some freezing rain to the greater Vancouver area beginning the night of the 11th continuing through the 14th. This was mostly a snow event for Clark County, with snowfall amounts ranging from around 6-10 inches downtown and East Vancouver, to up to 16 inches in northern portions of the zone. Ice accumulations up to 0.5 inch was also reported in south Vancouver. Widespread transportation delays and accidents occurred, including along I-5 and I-205. Scattered power outages were also reported. Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents, scattered power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Given the reports of 10-20 inches of snow in the Gorge, and over two feet around Mount Hood, it seems reasonable that the South Washington Cascades would see similar totals or more.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The south Washington Cascade foothills observed heavy snowfall. A member of the public reported 11 inches in Yacolt, which is actually in far northern WAZ039 but close enough to serve as a proxy for the South Washington Cascade foothills zone.  It is likely areas closer to the Columbia Gorge received even more snow than the Yacolt report.

As multiple Pacific fronts moved over the area, a major elevation winter storm with heavy snow and freezing rain occurred over a multi-day period. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and wind continued after the Blizzard Warning was converted to a Winter Storm Warning.  Storm total snowfall was around 24 inches at Cascade Locks by midday the 13th, and more arrived with the next impulse on the 14th and 15th. State Highway SR 14 was closed multiple days due to the heavy snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough drove an arctic airmass into southwest Washington. As multiple Pacific fronts moved over the area, a major elevation winter storm with heavy snow and freezing rain occurred over a multi-day period. This was a major, widespread, multi-faceted winter storm that caused major problems across the forecast area, even (and especially) in the lowlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snow shut down I-84 east of Troutdale.  This was a transition event, from blizzard to winter storm to freezing rain.  Mark Nelsen (KPTV Chief Met) reported 15 inches of snow total, followed by 0.75 of ice.  Sustained winds of 30-45 mph with gusts 60-80 mph were reported for 12-24 hours (or more), along with heavy snow reducing visibility to near zero during the height of the wind/snow portion of the event.  I-84 finally reopened the morning of the 14th as snow decreased and changed to a brief period of freezing rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wide variation of snow and ice accumulations, with impacts mainly felt near the Columbia River.  Cathlamet reported 12 inches of snow, while Astoria and Long Beach reported very little snow.  US Hwy 101 was closed at the Megler Bridge due to falling chunks of ice from the bridge structure.  Estimated ice near Astoria ranged from 0.33 to 0.66, and similar amounts can be assumed for the other side of the river.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell through the central Columbia Gorge between the night of the 11th through the 14th. Trained spotters at Underwood and Willard measured 10-20 inches of snow. State Highway SR 14 was closed multiple days.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 6.5 inches of snow in Ryderwood, Cowlitz County. A trained spotter report of 14.5 inches in the hills 4 miles NW of Castle Rock.  This is close enough to assume the Willapa Hills received similar totals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent fetch over the northeast Pacific Ocean generated large swell that reached the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cape Elizabeth buoy (46041) reported wave heights exceeding 23 feet at 15 seconds at 2140PST on the 25th. Wave heights peaked near 27 feet at 16 seconds at 2340PST on the 26th before dropping below 18 feet after 1540PST on the 26th.

A pier sunk, and several boats were pulled loose from moorings, with one floating adrift.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several weather systems moved across Western Washington at the beginning of the new year, bringing with them widespread lowland rain, heavy mountain snow, and gusty winds to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winter weather affected the Central Cascades at the beginning of the New Year. About 15 inches of new snow fell at Snoqualmie Pass beginning around noon on January 2nd through the evening hours on January 3. During the evening hours of January 2, I-90 EB was closed at MP 46 due to a spun out semi truck. Stevens Pass also experienced heavy snow during this event, with around 18-19 inches of snow within a 24 hour period extending from roughly 1PM January 2 through 1PM January 3. This prompted US 2 to close in both directions between MP 72 and MP 80 given hazardous road conditions.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cyclonic flow encompassed much of the lower 48 on February 6th, stretching from the Pacific Northwest through New England. Western Washington still remained in an active flow pattern, with the polar jet stream overtop the area, contributing to unsettled weather conditions and continued heavy mountain snow. The mountains affected the most in this unsettled pattern included the Central and Southern Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling around daybreak on February 6th and continued through the morning hours on February 7th, heavy at times. In a 24 hour period spanning roughly from 8am on February 6th to 8am on February 7th, around 19 inches of new snow fell at Stevens Pass.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cyclonic flow was present over the Pacific Northwest on February 25, with a strong jet streak in place at 250mb to aid in ascent as several small perturbations affected the region within the strong cyclonic flow. This would be sufficient to help enhance snowfall over the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow noticeably began to pick up across the Central Cascades shortly after midnight on February 25th and continued through daybreak on the 26th. During a 24 hour period from roughly 7am on the 25th to 7am on the 26th, Stevens Pass reported around 20 inches if new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong Fraser outflow over the north interior of Western Washington continued to intensify during the day on February 11. As a result, strong and gusty winds developed over portions of Western Whatcom and San Juan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were reported around Sandy Point Shores on February 11 in strong Fraser outflow out of the northeast. Gusts were reported by observation sites C1394 and KWAFERND4 of 71 and 72 mph, respectively. These gusty winds also made for cold morning wind chills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After heavy January precipitation, a large rock slide occurred on February 2nd when the upper section of a rock slope fell. A portion of US-2 in Pine Canyon, between Orondo and Waterville, were closed due to the debris covered roadway. The rock slide pulled the cable netting and sheared off a cable anchor. The clean up took over a month to complete.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington Department of Highways reported a large rock slide on US-2 in Pine Canyon. The debris covered approximately 300 yards, which included many large rocks. The highway was closed from MP140 near Orondo to MP148 near Waterville. There was no known time of reopening the road at time of report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 22nd and with the passage of a strong cold front, an afternoon of moderate rainfall brought 0.25 to 0.50 inches of precipitation to portions of Whitman County. The additional rain contributed to a culvert failure along State Route 23, south of Lamont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A culvert failed leading to mud, water, and debris over State Route 23, at MP33 south of Lamont. The road was closed for clean up. No other reports of damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough centered over Canada led to over a foot of snow at the Cascade Crest and mountain passes, especially US-2 and Stevens Pass, late February 6th and 7th. Tight pressure gradients created winds that reached gusts of 20 to 35 mph on the Eastern Cascade slope. This produced blowing snow and contributed to the hazardous driving conditions and poor visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington Department of Transportation reported that Stevens Pass received 18 inches of new snow accumulation in 24 hours. Heavy snow led to limited visibility and poor road conditions. US Highway 2 was closed due to spinouts, dangerous travel, and to perform avalanche control.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow and winds to eastern Washington on February 14th and 15th. Winds reached gusts of 20-30 mph and produced blowing snow, especially in the Palouse. Heavy morning snow had a significant impact on the commute, adding to already snow covered roads and creating poor visibility. Throughout the day, numerous accidents occurred on Interstate 90 in Spokane County and on US-2 near Stevens Pass. There were widespread snowfall across the region, but the greatest snow accumulations were reported across the eastern Cascades, Washington Palouse, and Spokane County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Spokane area received 4 to 6 inches of heavy snow. Most snow fell overnight, but lingering snow continued during the day. Snow accumulation and poor visibility on Interstate 90 and local roadways had a significant impact on the morning commute. The Washington State Patrol reported eight crashes on I-90 in just 45 minutes. Poor driving conditions continued into the evening commute, but not as severe as in the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow and winds to eastern Washington on February 14th and 15th. Winds reached gusts of 20-30 mph and produced blowing snow, especially in the Palouse. Heavy morning snow had a significant impact on the commute, adding to already snow covered roads and creating poor visibility. Throughout the day, numerous accidents occurred on Interstate 90 in Spokane County and on US-2 near Stevens Pass. There were widespread snowfall across the region, but the greatest snow accumulations were reported across the eastern Cascades, Washington Palouse, and Spokane County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Pomeroy area overnight February 14th into the early morning hours on the 15th. Several CoCoRaHS and trained spotters reported 5 to 7 inches of new snow accumulation by 8AM PST.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several weather systems moved across Western Washington at the beginning of the new year, bringing with them widespread lowland rain, heavy mountain snow, and gusty winds to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began to fall in the North Cascades during the early morning hours of January 2 and continued to fall before tapering around sunrise on January 3. NWAC snowfall observations indicate new snowfall amounts of around 20-25 inches near the Mount Baker Ski Area within this event.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several weather systems moved across Western Washington at the beginning of the new year, bringing with them widespread lowland rain, heavy mountain snow, and gusty winds to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began to fall in the Southern Cascades during the early afternoon hours on January 2. By 12 PM on January 3 (24 hours later), 21-22 inches of new snow were reported around Paradise at Mount Rainier National Park. Snow then gradually tapering into the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak weather system (followed quickly by several stronger systems) brought dangerous beach conditions and high surf to the Coast on from January 10-12. These dangerous beach conditions claimed the life of a woman on January 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grays Harbor County Sheriff's Office reported a fatality near the Pacific Beach State Park around 10:30 AM on January 10. A 54 year old woman from Oak Harbor, Washington was killed after being struck by a log on the beach. She and her husband were walking on the beach when a large set of waves came in and they stood on top of the log to avoid the waves. A wave knocked both of them of the log and rolled over them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow and winds to eastern Washington on February 14th and 15th. Winds reached gusts of 20-30 mph and produced blowing snow, especially in the Palouse. Heavy morning snow had a significant impact on the commute, adding to already snow covered roads and creating poor visibility. Throughout the day, numerous accidents occurred on Interstate 90 in Spokane County and on US-2 near Stevens Pass. There were widespread snowfall across the region, but the greatest snow accumulations were reported across the eastern Cascades, Washington Palouse, and Spokane County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and breezy winds created blowing snow and dangerous road conditions across the Washington Palouse. Widespread snow fell - from 4 inches in Oakesdale, 8 inches in Pullman, and 12 inches in Dusty. Winds, with gusts to 35 mph, created blowing snow, which limited visibility and contributed to poor driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought heavy snow and winds to eastern Washington on February 14th and 15th. Winds reached gusts of 20-30 mph and produced blowing snow, especially in the Palouse. Heavy morning snow had a significant impact on the commute, adding to already snow covered roads and creating poor visibility. Throughout the day, numerous accidents occurred on Interstate 90 in Spokane County and on US-2 near Stevens Pass. There were widespread snowfall across the region, but the greatest snow accumulations were reported across the eastern Cascades, Washington Palouse, and Spokane County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall, over 72 hours, brought severe and dangerous conditions across the Cascades, especially on US-2 and Stevens Pass. From 6AM February 14th through 6AM February 17th, 40 of snow fell at the pass. This contributed to numerous closures of on U-2 at Stevens Pass due to vehicle spinouts, avalanche control, and clean up. Breezy winds in the Cascades contributed to blowing snow and poor visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a cold front on February 22nd lead to lower snow levels and heavy snowfall in the Cascades. Over a foot of snow fell in the Cascade Crest from February 22nd evening through the 23rd. With the frontal pass, winds were breezy with gusts reaching 20-35 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several bands of heavy snow closed Stevens Pass at 6PM February 22nd through 8AM on February 23rd. The Washington Department of Transportation reported 11 inches of new snow accumulation at Stevens Pass. Heavy snow continued falling through the day adding to already dangerous driving conditions. The Northwest Avalanche Center reported very dangerous avalanche conditions and urged people to avoid backcountry travel due to strong winds and the ongoing snow. There are no reports of injuries due to avalanches.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact shortwave trough, aided by very strong flow aloft passed across Western Washington on February 22, bringing with it another round of heavy snowfall to the Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began in the Central Cascades on February 21st and peaked in intensity on February 22nd. In a 12 hour period spanning from 6am to 6pm on February 22nd, around 15 inches of new snow fell across Stevens Pass. Snow then tapered into the night, with a few more inches reported by sunrise on the 23rd. In total, around 19 inches of new snow fell in a 24 hour period spanning from 6am on the 22nd to 6am on the 23rd.||In addition to heavy snowfall, the presence of high avalanche danger amidst an unstable snowpack caused WSDOT to to close all major cross-cascade highways, including US 2 over Stevens Pass and I-90 over Snoqualmie Pass the evening of February 21st through the morning of February 22nd. After opening the morning of the 22nd, additional snowfall then led WSDOT to re-close US 2 over Stevens Pass. Avalanches were reported near the Pass after the highway closed, along with reports of snow slides blocking the road on the east side of the Pass between MP 58 and 85. The highway did not reopen until February 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was another round of mountain snow and winds across the eastern Cascades on February 25th and 26th. Additional snow pack in the Cascades, with the strong winds, created further avalanche danger and it was urged to avoid backcountry travel. Ice accumulation on roads and surfaces created hazardous travel conditions and road closures. The snow continued into the evening of February 26th, but much lighter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was heavy snowfall and ice coverage in the eastern Cascades, which led to pass closures, dangerous road conditions, and snow slides. US-2 Highway at Stevens Pass was closed on February 25th night due to the slide which covered the roadway. There were 27 inches of new snow accumulation at the pass on February 25th and 26th. The Washington Department of Transportation reported ice covered power lines were hanging less than 12 feet from the roads and led to further closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough with mid to high level jet support moved northeast across Oregon and Washington, producing showers with embedded strong thunderstorms during the early morning hours and in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media report 14 miles NE of Dayton, WA indicated a thunderstorm produced hail up to the size of 1.25 inches at 12:45 am PDT on June 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level trough with mid to high level jet support moved northeast across Oregon and Washington, producing showers with embedded strong thunderstorms during the early morning hours and in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Walla Walla county Sheriff confirmed that a thunderstorm producing gusty winds had  uprooted several trees, downed power lines, and had flipped a small plane that was not tied down at Martin Airfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and shortwave trough moving across the region Sunday, March 28th produced gusty winds with blowing dust across much of the forecast area. Winds were strong enough to cause power outages in portions of Kennewick, WA, while low visibility from blowing dust contributed to a 7 car accident in Richland, WA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station 8 miles SSE of Goldendale, WA measured a non-tstm wind gust of 84 mph at 7:13pm PDT on March 28th. A mesonet station 22 miles NE of Goldendale, WA measured a non-tstm wind gust of 76 mph at 1:56pm PDT on March 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front produced strong winds in eastern Washington on March 28th. Winds reached gusts of 50-65 mph. These powerful winds produced blowing dust in the dry Columbia Basin and damage due to downed trees in much of far eastern Washington. Blowing dust had significant impact on travel creating poor visibility on major interstates and highways. The strongest winds and damage occurred in the late afternoon and early evening which created hazardous roadways for the evening commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and dry conditions resulted in blowing dust and near zero visibility in southeastern Douglas County. A state trooper reported that an eight vehicle pile-up, with minor injuries, occurred along State Route 26, near Othello. Blowing dust led to hazardous driving conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Transportation closed an approximate 30 mile stretch of Interstate 90 in Adams County due to dangerous, life threatening travel conditions. There was near zero visibility from blowing dust across the interstate. Wind gusts reached 45 to 55 mph at nearby RAWS and COOP stations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer in Thorton measured a 59 mph maximum wind gust on March 28th. The time is an estimate based on other peak winds in the area.

Blowing dust had significant impact on travel creating poor visibility on major interstates and highways. The strongest winds and damage occurred in the late afternoon and early evening which created hazardous roadways for the evening commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across the upper Columbia Basin many observation sites measured wind gusts from 40-50 mph. The peak winds occurred in northern Lincoln County at the Keller Ferry site. The ferry on State Route 21 was closed due to gusts of 60 mph and higher at the final crossing. There were no reports of damage or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front produced strong winds in eastern Washington on March 28th. Winds reached gusts of 50-65 mph. These powerful winds produced blowing dust in the dry Columbia Basin and damage due to downed trees in much of far eastern Washington. Blowing dust had significant impact on travel creating poor visibility on major interstates and highways. The strongest winds and damage occurred in the late afternoon and early evening which created hazardous roadways for the evening commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds from a passing cold front created dangerous driving conditions in the dry Columbia Basin. The peak wind gusts reached 40-50 mph across the area, which included Moses Lake, Ephrata, and Othello. A significant portion of Interstate 90 and State Route 26 were closed due to blowing dust. The reduced visibility on SR-26, southeast of Othello, caused an eight car pile up. There were two, non-life threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front moved across the Cascade Mountains on March 28th. With the passing front, snow levels dropped drastically bringing conditions prime for heavy snow at the Cascade Crest in the late afternoon and overnight. Significant snow accumulations began as temperatures dropped and roadways cooled. The gusty winds associated with this front created blowing snow reducing visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Department of Transportation measured 14 inches of snow at Stevens Pass on March 28th. At the Cascade Crest on Highway 2, there were reports of light-to-heavy snow and blowing snow due to gusty winds. This led to hazardous road conditions and avalanche control planned for the next day. There are no reports of accidents or injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front produced strong winds in eastern Washington on March 28th. Winds reached gusts of 50-65 mph. These powerful winds produced blowing dust in the dry Columbia Basin and damage due to downed trees in much of far eastern Washington. Blowing dust had significant impact on travel creating poor visibility on major interstates and highways. The strongest winds and damage occurred in the late afternoon and early evening which created hazardous roadways for the evening commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Spokane recorded a wind gust of 58 mph. There were many other local sites in Spokane County with 45-50 mph gusts. The strong winds downed many large trees across the county causing power lines down, power outages, and hazardous roadways to due blockages. There was extensive damage in northern Spokane, including two homes were damaged from downed trees.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting spring storm system brought heavy snow to the Cascades at the end of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall in the North Cascades around noon on March 28. Within a 12 hour period, 24 inches of new snow was recorded at the Mount Baker Ski Area. Snow then continued through the early morning hours on the 29th before ending.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting spring storm system brought heavy snow to the Cascades at the end of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began around midday on the 28th across the Central Cascades and within 12 hours, 13 inches of new snow was recorded at Stevens Pass. Snow then continued through the morning hours of the 29th before coming to an end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accompanying an atmospheric river, a strong winter storm system brought periods of moderate to heavy snow on January 11th and 12th. The northern Cascades, eastern Cascade valleys, and Okanogan Highlands received the greatest snow totals, with a two day snow accumulation of 17.5 at Stevens Pass. Four to eight inches new snow accumulations were common throughout the remainder of the impacted areas. With saturated soils and snow covered branches, these conditions added to the strong frontal winds arriving January 13th causing isolated power outages and fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer, 12 miles northwest of Entiat, recorded 7.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly upper level jet over the northern Pacific was directed at the Washington and Oregon coast, driving a plume of deep moisture toward western Washington and northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Willapa River at Willapa (WILW1) reached flood stage at 0345PST, crested at 21.2 feet then dropped below flood stage at 0800PST on January 3rd.  Flood stage is 21.0 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day snow event lead to heavy snow accumulations from late January 26th into the early hours on the 28th. The first mid-level wave brought snow to most areas into the early afternoon January 27th. Enhanced lift brought a boundary of heavy snow from the Wenatchee area into the Okanogan Valley and across southern Ferry, Stevens, and Pend Oreille counties. Morning reports showed snow accumulations of 4-8 inches fell across that area. Light snow, with accumulations 1-3 inches, fell across much of the remaining eastern Washington low lands. ||The second mid-level wave arrived the evening of January 27th and departed by the early morning hours of the 28th. Not quite a strong as the first, this next round of snowfall accumulated another 3-6 inches across much of the northern Washington counties. Two day snow total ranged from 5 inches in the valleys to 8 to 10 inches in higher elevations of Cascades and northeastern WA mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mission Ridge Ski Resort reported 8.0 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through eastern Washington on the morning of January 13th.  Following moderate mountain snow and valley rain, severe winds raced through the region. Widespread gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed across the Columbia Basin, with 60 to 70 mph winds observed in the Spokane and Coeur d���Alene region. The Spokane International Airport tied its second strongest all time wind speed record of 71 mph.  ||There was widespread damage, downed trees, and power outages throughout much of the Spokane area, while relatively isolated in the remainder of the region. At the height of the storm, an approximate 103,000 households throughout the region were without power. Numerous school districts cancelled school for the day due to lack of power. Overall, it took a week to restore power to all who lost it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported one fatality when a large tree fell on a car and trapped the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through eastern Washington on the morning of January 13th.  Following moderate mountain snow and valley rain, severe winds raced through the region. Widespread gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed across the Columbia Basin, with 60 to 70 mph winds observed in the Spokane and Coeur d���Alene region. The Spokane International Airport tied its second strongest all time wind speed record of 71 mph.  ||There was widespread damage, downed trees, and power outages throughout much of the Spokane area, while relatively isolated in the remainder of the region. At the height of the storm, an approximate 103,000 households throughout the region were without power. Numerous school districts cancelled school for the day due to lack of power. Overall, it took a week to restore power to all who lost it.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington Department of Transportation reported State Route 20 south bound lanes blocked due to trees down near Usk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist frontal system arrived overnight into the early morning hours of January 4th. A heavy, wet snow fell in the Cascades and northeastern Washington mountains. The significant snow accumulations fell in the Methow Valley and eastern Cascades, which received 4-6 inches over approximately 12 hours. On a saturated soil, this wet snow led to a mudslide onto State Route 2 between Peshastin and Leavenworth. Due to mild temperatures, most of the region received precipitation in the form of rain, or a change from light snow to rain. The system was out of the region by the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter, 7 miles northwest of Malott, recorded 4.5 inches of new snow accumulation. It was still snowing at time of report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accompanying an atmospheric river, a strong winter storm system brought periods of moderate to heavy snow on January 11th and 12th. The northern Cascades, eastern Cascade valleys, and Okanogan Highlands received the greatest snow totals, with a two day snow accumulation of 17.5 at Stevens Pass. Four to eight inches new snow accumulations were common throughout the remainder of the impacted areas. With saturated soils and snow covered branches, these conditions added to the strong frontal winds arriving January 13th causing isolated power outages and fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Berne WSDOT Camp recorded 15.8 inches of new snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter systems moved through the region starting Thursday Jan. 11th through Saturday Jan. 13th, and produced moderate to heavy snow across much of the forecast area. Persistent northwest flow aloft also sustained snow showers across the mountains between the systems. The second system was far more productive, with many lower elevation areas seeing 4-7 inches and locally higher of additional accumulating snow during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media report originating 5 miles ENE of Walla Walla, WA estimated 8.5 inches of snowfall over a 48 hour period ending at 10:21am PST on Feb. 13th. A local newspaper from Walla Walla, WA estimated 5 inches of snowfall in a 24 hour period ending at 2:00pm PST on Feb. 12th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cocorahs observer in SSE Prosser, WA measured 10.5 inches of snowfall in a 24 hour period ending at 9:30am PST on Feb. 13th. A social media report originating from Richland, WA measured 10 inches of snowfall at 9:05am on Feb. 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy Snow affected the Blue Mountain Foothills of WA February 14-15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dayton reported 12 inches of snow.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave trough of low pressure passed across Western Washington at the beginning of February and then began depart the area on February 4th. In the shortwave's wake was the development of an unstable atmosphere, with strong flow aloft and lingering upper level divergence in the left exit region of a strong jet. This resulted in heavy snowfall over the mountains from February 4 through February 5, affecting mostly the Central Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began to fall around Stevens Pass and the nearby Central Cascades around day break on February 4th and began to pick up in intensity around 6PM. From the evening hours of February 4th to daybreak on February 5th ( a 12 hour period), around 14 inches of new snow fell. Snow then tapered through the remaining hours of the morning on the 5th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cyclonic flow encompassed much of the lower 48 on February 6th, stretching from the Pacific Northwest through New England. Western Washington still remained in an active flow pattern, with the polar jet stream overtop the area, contributing to unsettled weather conditions and continued heavy mountain snow. The mountains affected the most in this unsettled pattern included the Central and Southern Cascades.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began near daybreak on February 6th and continued through the early morning hours of February 8th. Snow was heaviest on the 6th, from around sunrise through midnight. In a 12 hour period from roughly 8am to 8pm on February 6th, Paradise at Mount Rainier picked up around 14 inches of new snow.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cyclonic flow continued over much of the lower 48 in mid February, with Western Washington experiencing much colder than normal conditions. Between February 16 and February 17, heavy snow affected the mountains, particularly the Central Cascades as a surface area of low pressure passed across the area, accompanied by some support in the left exit region of a 250mb jet streak over Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of snowfall occurred across the Cascades in the middle of February. Snow began falling on February 14th, increasing in intensity on February 15th. Then, between the hours of 6am on February 16th and 6am on February 17th snowfall peaked in intensity around Stevens Pass, with 20 inches of new reported during this time period. Snoqualmie Pass also experienced heavy snow during this prolonged event. Snowfall at Snoqualmie Pass peaked in intensity on the 15th, with 11 inches of new snow reported between 6am to 6pm on the 15th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted all of Western Washington during the overnight period on February 12 and continuing through the morning hours of February 13. While most of the Washington lowlands experienced significant snowfall, the Washington Coast experienced freezing rain and ice accumulation as a layer of warmer air over spread the very cold arctic airmass at the surface. This would prompt the second ice storm warning in Western Washington history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours on February 12th, reports from the public began to come in regarding significant (at least 1/4) ice accumulation on many surfaces. Areas from around Ocean Shores to Pacific reportedly received the highest amounts of ice accumulation. Precipitation would then move more inland/to the east during the later morning hours, ending the highest chances for ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant snowstorm impacted the lowlands of Washington beginning during the evening hours of February 12 and continuing through the morning of February 13. Strong Fraser outflow was responsible for the advection of much colder air west of the Cascade Crest. As this cold air entrenched the Western Washington lowlands, a strong storm system was approaching from off the Pacific, bringing with it sufficient moisture towards the area. The low made landfall near the mouth of the Columbia River during the early morning hours of the 13th, which was the prime location to allow for significant snowfall over Western Washington. Heavy snowfall accumulated quickly overnight and continued through the morning of the 13th before tapering during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to spread across Western Whatcom County during the late overnight period of February 12 into February 13th. Local CocoRaHs observations by daybreak on the 13th showed generally 3-5 inches of new snow across the western portion of the county. Snow would then taper through the afternoon, with generally minimal additional accumulation.

Local CoCoRaHs reports around daybreak on February 13 ranged from generally 5-10 inches of snow through that short time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust cold front, accompanied by strong north to northeast winds, passed through eastern Washington on April 18th. Evening winds reached gusts in the range of 40-60 mph. The current drought, with loose field soils, produced the prime conditions for blowing dust from the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Mesonet station in Beverly observed wind gusts 30-40 mph. There were reports of several broken power poles and power lines covering streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 14 year old male was swept out to sea while swimming near Long Beach, WA. United States Coast Guard performed search and rescue, but has since suspended the search.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 14 year old male was reportedly swept offshore while swimming near Long Beach, Washington.  The Coast Guard Sector Columbia River and partner agencies performed search operations. After searching nearly 52 square miles, the search was suspended at|2126PST Monday.  Weather was fairly benign with general seas between 3 and 5 feet with northerly winds around 9 to 13 kts gusting  up to 17 kts. These reports are from buoy 46029.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed onshore on the morning which brought high winds to the coastal areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed onshore on the morning which brought high winds to the coastal areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station in Yacolt recorded a wind gust to 40 mph. Other wind gusts recorded were 35 mph at Jantzen Beach at 1241PST, 32 mph at Heisson at 1237PST, 30 mph at Pearson Field Airport (KVUO) at 1206PST, and 32 mph at Battle Ground at 1338PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with elevated river levels lead to minor tidal over flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tidal overflow flooding occurred in the late morning through early afternoon hours on 4th through 6th.  Waters covered fields around Raymond, WA. The water did not rise high enough to close highway 101. The Toke Point tide gage was used to determine the time the water level rose above 11 feet, the point at which tidal overflow usually begins. The start time was the first time it rose above 11 feet on the 4th and the end time was the last time it dropped below 11 feet on the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accompanying an atmospheric river, a strong winter storm system brought periods of moderate to heavy snow late January 11th and 12th. The northern Cascades, eastern Cascade valleys, and Okanogan Highlands received the greatest snow totals. With high snow levels, most precipitation fell as rain across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heaviest snow fell in the northern Cascades and eastern Cascade valleys overnight Monday, January 11th, into early Tuesday afternoon. The Cascade valleys received 6 to 10 inches of new snow accumulation. The mountains and passes received 12 to 15 inches. With saturated soils and snow covered branches, the heavy snow caused isolated power outages and fallen trees. The Chelan County Public Utility District reported widespread outages in the county with most restored by the afternoon of January 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust cold front, accompanied by strong north to northeast winds, passed through eastern Washington on April 18th. Evening winds reached gusts in the range of 40-60 mph. The current drought, with loose field soils, produced the prime conditions for blowing dust from the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds led to moderate blowing dust, widespread trees down, and property damage in Green Acres. The peak wind gust of 60 mph is estimated based on damage reports. Across southern Spokane, the winds downed many large trees causing power lines down, power outages, and hazardous roadways to due blockages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains focused over the Willapa Hills in southwest Washington brought minor flooding to the Grays River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grays River crested at 12.5 feet on the afternoon of November 5th. Flood stage is 12 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains produced by an atmospheric river brought river flooding to portions of northwest Oregon and southwest Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cowlitz River at Kelso (KELW1) rose above flood stage on the morning of the 11th, created at 21.5 feet around 2000PST on the 11th, then fell below flood stage on the morning of the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust cold front, accompanied by strong north to northeast winds, passed through eastern Washington on April 18th. Evening winds reached gusts in the range of 40-60 mph. The current drought, with loose field soils, produced the prime conditions for blowing dust from the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Motorists driving from Lake Roosevelt to Spokane encountered several areas of 1/16 mile visibility or less due to blowing dust. This occurred on both U.S. Highway 2 between Davenport and Deep Creek and Washington State Route 25 north of Davenport. There were no reports of accidents due to the near zero visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust cold front, accompanied by strong north to northeast winds, passed through eastern Washington on April 18th. Evening winds reached gusts in the range of 40-60 mph. The current drought, with loose field soils, produced the prime conditions for blowing dust from the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and blowing dust made for a difficult task fighting a small brush fire off Palouse Highway in southern Spokane. There were many power lines and trees down across the roads with blowing dust resulting in near zero visibility. There were no fatalities or injuries. No lost property or evacuations needed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough's arrival led to tightening pressure gradients and gusty winds in eastern Washington on April 13th. Daytime winds reached gusts in the range of 30-40 mph across the Waterville Plateau, Moses Lake Area, and Upper Columbia Basin. Fields were in the season where top soil is more easily blown around, drought conditions persist, and little recent rainfall across this area. These conditions, with the strong winds, led to widespread blowing dust across the Columbia Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and dry conditions resulted in blowing dust and near zero visibility in southern Adams County. A National Weather Service employee reported blowing dust on US-395 south of Ritzville. There were numerous walls of dust across State Route 26, from Hatton Coulee to Othello, with zero visibility. No known accidents or injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 62 mph was recorded at the Wenatchee Airport. Local utilities reported power outages in parts of the Wenatchee area due to wind damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy, wet snow fell overnight into the early morning hours of January 4th. The significant snow accumulations fell in the Methow Valley and eastern Cascades, which received 4 to 6 inches over approximately 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough brought cool and windy conditions to eastern Washington on May 23rd. Sustained winds remained elevated through the day with gusty winds in the |afternoon and evening across the Columbia Basin and eastern Cascade slopes. Peak gusts reached 40 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds, measured up to 50 mph, occurred through the Eastern Cascade slopes into the upper Columbia Basin. There were public reports and photos of roof damage and broken tree limbs in Chelan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passage brought strengthening and shifting winds on May 27th. Gusts reached speeds of 40 to 55 mph across the Interstate 90 and US Highway 2 corridors. The current drought, with loose field soils, produced the prime conditions for blowing dust from the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds, with gusts from 40 to 55 mph, led to blowing dust and near zero visibility in Odessa. There were no reports of accidents in the area. Avista Utilities reported power outages in the Odessa area around the time of peak winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passage brought strengthening and shifting winds on May 27th. Gusts reached speeds of 40 to 55 mph across the Interstate 90 and US Highway 2 corridors. The current drought, with loose field soils, produced the prime conditions for blowing dust from the strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds, with gusts up to 50 mph, and dry conditions led to numerous dust storms throughout the afternoon in the Columbia Basin. The Department of Highways reported significant blowing dust with near zero visibility across roadways. Decreasing visibility contributed to multiple collisions on Interstate 90 around Moses Lake. This led to road closures for several hours. Avista Utilities reported power outages in the Othello area around the time of peak winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through eastern Washington on the morning of January 13th.  Following moderate mountain snow and valley rain, severe winds raced through the region. Widespread gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed across the Columbia Basin, with 60 to 70 mph winds observed in the Spokane and Coeur d���Alene region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind storm, with 60 to 70 mph observed winds, impacted the Spokane and Coeur d���Alene region on the morning of January 13th. The Spokane International Airport tied its second strongest all time wind speed record of 71 mph. This led to extensive damage in the Spokane area and the neighborhoods south of Interstate 90 were the hardest hit. The total home damage is unknown and damage totals is estimated, much due to downed pine trees and power poles. There were numerous reports of downed power lines and poles on Interstate 90, which blocked interstate lanes and on ramps. One fatality occurred when a large tree fell on a car and trapped the driver. Additionally, a tree fell on a home and trapped an occupant in bed. They were extricated and hospitalized for a crush injury.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hillside along State Route 2 began slowly sliding, striking the Department of Transportation guardrail, and partially blocked the eastbound lane. The mudslide was triggered by already saturated soil and wet snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an active day of low topped supercells over the coastal waters, one moved ashore producing a tornado in Ilwaco, Washington during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado occurred in Ilwaco, Washington just north of the Port of Ilwaco Airport. The tornado began near the intersection of Redwing Way and Captain Gray Drive around 1904PST, tracked northeast across a housing subdivision, crossed the Wallacut River before dissipating near the intersection of Chinook Valley Road and U.S. Highway 101 around 1908PST.  The tornado track was about one-third of a mile long and 60 yards wide.  There was minor damage to trees, fences, windows, shingles, and other unsecured attachments to structures along the path (DI 2, DOD 2).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dome of hot high pressure over the Inland Northwest pushed afternoon high temperatures into record territory. In late June 2021, a long-lived heatwave broke many daily, monthly, and all time maximum high and low temperature records at several stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Republic reached an all time record high temperature of 109 degrees on the afternoon of June 28th. On June 30th, there was a report, from the Washington State Department of Transportation, that there was damage to road surfaces at Sherman Pass due to the heat. Local libraries were arranged to act as cooling centers across the area. There are no known heat related deaths.

Local broadcast news reported that, near Inspiration Lake, a hiker was rescued after suffering from heat exposure. Local libraries were arranged to act as cooling centers across the area. There are no known heat related deaths.

Omak reached an all time record high temperature of 117 degrees on the afternoon of June 29th. Local libraries were arranged to act as cooling centers for those with out air conditioning across the area. A report from the Washington Department of Health stated that there were four heat-related deaths in Okanogan County. Further specifics were statewide and not given by counties.

Local cooling centers were set up by emergency management for those without air conditioning across the area. A report from the Washington Department of Health stated that there were two heat-related deaths in Douglas County. Further specifics were statewide and not given by counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dome of hot high pressure over the Inland Northwest pushed afternoon high temperatures into record territory. In late June 2021, a long-lived heatwave broke many daily, monthly, and all time maximum high and low temperature records at several stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of excessive heat left numerous heat-related impacts and new all time record high maximum temperatures in the Spokane Metropolitan area. Spokane International Airport broke their record when they observed an afternoon high of 109 degrees on June 28th. The record warmest low temperature followed on June 30th when only cooling to 78 degrees. Avista Utilities performed two blackouts due to the heat which impacted approximately 8300 customers. There are eight known heat-related deaths in Spokane County. There are no other known specifics about the fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge of high pressure and a surface thermal trough brought several days of record high temperatures across the PacNW, with many locations in the lower and higher elevations experiencing extreme heat risk during this event. The multiple days of extreme heat risk has been attributed to several fatalities across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The GHCN station for Kennewick, WA recorded consecutive days between June 27th through July 1st of temperatures that met or exceeded excessive heat warning criteria. Several fatalities across Benton and Franklin counties were attributed to the heat wave during this period.

The Yakima county Coroner' Office reported 4 fatalities that heat was a contributing factor to during the heat wave, however, no additional details were provided regarding age, sex, actual date, or location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The period from January 10 to 16 continued a pattern of a strong and persistent Aleutian Low pressure system in the eastern Pacific that began December 29, 2020 and which directed the jet stream and primary storm track at Washington State.  This resulted in a series of atmospheric rivers and wind storms that battered Washington State. Water vapor transport into Washington State averaged 160-200% of normal during the period from December 29 through January 16. Numerous periods of heavy rain occurred during this period, which in turn resulted in widespread and sustained river flooding, urban and small stream flooding, mudslides, and landslides in addition to the significant and widespread impacts from high winds, pounding surf, and coastal flooding.||During the wettest portion of this 19-day period, Washington State experienced widespread precipitation totals between 200% and 350% of normal. The prolonged bouts of moderate to heavy rain between January 10 and 16, 2021, resulted in 12 major rivers exceeding flood stage, 3 of them exceeded moderate flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cowlitz River at Randle and Newaukum River near Chehalis exceeded flood stage. Urban and small stream flooding occurred also.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From December 29 - January 16, a strong and persistent Aleutian Low pressure system developed in the eastern Pacific which directed the jet stream and primary storm track at Washington State that resulted in a rapid series of atmospheric rivers and wind storms that battered Washington State. Water vapor transport into Washington State averaged 160-200% of normal during the period from December 29 through January 16. Numerous periods of heavy rain occurred during the episode, which in turn resulted in widespread and sustained river flooding, urban and small stream flooding, mudslides, and landslides in addition to the significant and widespread impacts from high winds, pounding surf, and coastal flooding.||During the wettest portion of this 19-day period, Washington State experienced widespread precipitation totals between 200% and 350% of normal. The prolonged bouts of moderate to heavy rain between December 29, 2020 and January 7, 2021, resulted in 8 major rivers exceeding flood stage; with 4 of them exceeding moderate flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chehalis River near Doty, Newaukum River near Centralia rose above flood stage Jan 3.  Urban and small stream flooding occurred as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: From December 29 - January 16, a strong and persistent Aleutian Low pressure system developed in the eastern Pacific which directed the jet stream and primary storm track at Washington State that resulted in a rapid series of atmospheric rivers and wind storms that battered Washington State. Water vapor transport into Washington State averaged 160-200% of normal during the period from December 29 through January 16. Numerous periods of heavy rain occurred during the episode, which in turn resulted in widespread and sustained river flooding, urban and small stream flooding, mudslides, and landslides in addition to the significant and widespread impacts from high winds, pounding surf, and coastal flooding.||During the wettest portion of this 19-day period, Washington State experienced widespread precipitation totals between 200% and 350% of normal. The prolonged bouts of moderate to heavy rain between December 29, 2020 and January 7, 2021, resulted in 8 major rivers exceeding flood stage; with 4 of them exceeding moderate flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Skookumchuck River near Bucoda and Chehalis River near Grand Mound exceeded moderate flood stage. Urban and small stream flooding occurred as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level trough produced northwesterly breezy conditions and gusts up to 60 mph across Washington during the afternoon of May 1, 2021.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-thunderstorm wind gust of 60mph was measured by the ASOS KELN in Ellensburg, WA at 2:01 pm PDT.

Numerous periods of heavy rain occurred during this period, which in turn resulted in widespread and sustained river flooding, urban and small stream flooding, mudslides, and landslides in addition to the significant and widespread impacts from high winds, pounding surf, and coastal flooding.||During the wettest portion of this 19-day period, Washington State experienced widespread precipitation totals between 200% and 350% of normal. The prolonged bouts of moderate to heavy rain between January 10 and 16, 2021, resulted in 12 major rivers exceeding flood stage, 3 of them exceeded moderate flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Satsop River near Satsop and Chehalis River at Porter exceeded flood stage. Urban and small stream flooding also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge of high pressure and a surface thermal trough brought several days of record high temperatures across the PacNW, with many locations in the lower and higher elevations experiencing extreme heat risk during this event. The multiple days of extreme heat risk has been attributed to several fatalities across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly all RAWS stations across the zone recorded consecutive daily high temperatures over 100 degrees through the period, while the Goldendale East RAWS site recorded a high temperature of 110 degrees on June 29th.

The multiple days of extreme heat risk has been attributed to several fatalities across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several RAWS stations across the zone recorded consecutive daily high temperatures over 95 degrees through the period, with the Sawmill Flats, Swauk, and Buck Creek RAWS sites having recorded consecutive daily high temperatures over 100 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept through eastern Washington on the morning of January 13th.  Following moderate mountain snow and valley rain, severe winds raced through the region. Widespread gusts of 50 to 60 mph were observed across the Columbia Basin, with 60 to 70 mph winds observed in the Spokane and Coeur d���Alene region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds led to US Highway 395 closure in both directions due to power pole and lines across the roadway near Clayton. Wind gusts are estimated based on wind damage and surrounding wind speed observations.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grain truck was blown over in a field, just north of Odessa. No one was driving at the time. The truck was empty and estimated to weigh 20,000 pounds. The wind gust speed is estimated based on the truck weight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure heat dome over the region led to stretch of extreme heat, shattering records from June 26 through June 29. Hot temperatures resulted in many people seeking locations to cool off in local rivers and beaches, which led to one drowning, as well as multiple people going to local hospitals for treatment of typical heat-related medical symptoms, including one reported death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures. A Mesonet Weather Station near Underwood observed three consecutive days with temperatures above 105 degrees. This station reported a maximum temperature of 107 on June 26, 113 on June 27, and 115 on June 28. The minimum temperatures were in the lower 70s on June 27 and June 28.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures. There were three consecutive days with maximum temperatures above 100 degrees. Maximum temperatures peaked around 108 degrees on June 27, and 112 degrees on June 28. The minimum temperatures were in the mid 70s on June 27 and June 28.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were reports of a power pole and lines down due to strong winds. Blowing dust reduced visibility across Highway 28 in Soap Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Red flag conditions, with very low humidity and breezy winds, led to the ideal set up for  wildfire ignition. In addition, the inaccessible terrain, with steep drainages and cliffs, made conditions more difficult to extinguish the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Batterman Road Fire, in eastern Wenatchee, started on July 4th and burned a total 14,100 acres of brush before it was contained on July 13th. The cause of ignition is unknown and under investigation by authorities. There were no injuries or structures lost. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at 6.6 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures, with very low relative humidity and dry fuels, provided dangerous conditions for rapid fire spread. There were wind gusts to 25 mph recorded at the Wenatchee Airport the day of fire ignition.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red Apple Fire started on July 13th and burned a total 12,288 acres before it was contained on July 25th. It began at the US-97 and US Highway 2 corridor, on the outskirts of Monitor. The cause of ignition has not been confirmed and under investigation by authorities. There were 400 structures threatened and numerous evacuations. There was one firefighter with a minor injury and five outbuildings lost in the fire. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at 4.7 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active thunderstorms moved over eastern Washington, especially Whitman County, late July 21st. The high lightning frequency, with numerous positive strikes, ignited the Steptoe Canyon Fire. High temperatures over 100 degrees and dry conditions in the days following fueled the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Steptoe Canyon Fire started on July 21st and burned a total 2,605 acres before it was contained on July 27th. The cause of ignition was due to lightning from thunderstorms that passed through the area that evening. There were homes, rangeland, and crops threatened. There are no known homes lost or injuries from the fire. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at 550,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Passing showers on the Waterville Plateau had embedded thunderstorms with a few lightning strikes and gusty winds. With the strong winds, hot conditions and dry fuels fueled the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whitehall Road Fire, near Coulee Meadows, started on July 8th and burned a total 800 acres before it was contained on July 10th. The fire was ignited by lightning with a passing evening thunderstorm. Local news reported that gusty winds created blackout conditions from blowing dust and fire spread in many directions. There are no known injuries or property loss. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at 300,000 dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced heavy rain across northern Washington, with 0.38 of rain measured in 2 hours at a site close to the debris flow reports. A flash flood warning was issued due to high intensity rainfall occurring in areas in and below the Crescent Mountain Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rock slide blocked both lanes of State Route 20 near mile post 166, two miles west of Cutthroat Creek Road. Heavy rain in the area lead to the slide and water over the roadway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The US Forest Service reported a debris flow that blocked the NF-4440 Road, one mile east of the Gilbert Trailhead. The flow was approximately four feet deep and stranded two cars overnight. Time is estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day snow event lead to heavy snow accumulations from late January 26th into the early hours on the 28th. The first mid-level wave brought snow to most areas into the early afternoon January 27th. Enhanced lift brought a boundary of heavy snow from the Wenatchee area into the Okanogan Valley and Methow Valley. The second mid-level wave arrived the evening of January 27th and departed by the early morning hours of the 28th. The snow was lighter than the first round.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two mid-level waves brought an impressive two day snow accumulation. Enhanced lift brought a boundary of heavy snow on January 27th. Light snow fell on the following day, but there was a two-day total snow of 9 to 11 inches in the Wenatchee area along the Columbia River.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two mid-level waves brought an impressive two-day snow accumulation. Enhanced lift brought a boundary of heavy snow on January 27th. Light snow fell on the following day, but there was a two-day total of 7 to 12 inches across the Methow Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boundary of heavy snow moved through eastern Washington late January 26th into the 27th. It brought a 24 hour total snow accumulation of 4 to 6 inches of snow in the southern Okanogan Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level shortwave with a modified arctic cold front brought moderate to heavy snow showers across the Cascades, Blues, and Wallowas, with moderate to heavy snow being observed along the Blue Mountain foothills, and in valley locations across the northeast Oregon mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Via a social media report from Walla Walla, WA, 13 inches of new snow was measured at 1200 PST on Dec.30th. Relayed from an Emergency Manager in Walla Walla, WA, 10 inches of new snow was measured at 0900 on Dec. 30th. Via a social media report in Walla Walla, WA, a member of the public measured an estimated 9 inches of new snow over a 11 hour period ending at 1300 PST on Dec. 30th. A member of the public in Dayton, WA measured 4 inches of new snow over a 24 hour period ending at 1300 PST on Dec. 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific front caused high winds along the south Washington coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were reported along the south Washington coast. Max sustained winds of 55 mph with a gust to 68 mph was recorded at Megler Bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first major winter storm of the season dumped over a foot of snow in the Cascades. Much of the snow fell on the 11th in the higher elevations, then snow levels lowered enough to produce significant accumulations in many areas above 2500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow fell in the south Washington Cascades between December 10 and 13, with accumulations of 18 to 28 inches of snow reported. During this time there was a 12 hour period where 10 to 16 inches of snow fell early on the 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system and strong cold front moved through the Pacific Northwest on the evening of December 25, 2021 through the day on December 26th, 2021. This storm brought a significant amount of snow throughout the Pacific Northwest, including along the south Washington coast, but especially in the Cascades. Snow resulted in significant travel issues for the holiday weekend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 1 to 5 inches of snowfall.
