EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough Gulf Low continued off the west coast that brought a series of atmospheric rivers resulting in flooding across parts of western Washington. This second atmospheric river episode of the month was a set of three atmospheric rivers back-to-back-to-back. Water vapor transport into Western Washington averaged 160 - 200% of normal during the period of 11 November - 16 November. Heavy rainfall spread across the region on the night of the 11th through November 12, with the heaviest rain starting across Southwest Washington and slowly shifting to the north through the day. Rainfall totals ranged from 5 to 10 inches in spots leading to areal and river flooding, including major flooding. |Heavy, persistent rainfall associated with the Atmospheric River shifted northward across Western Washington on November 13 before stalling over northwest Washington November 14 through 16. Another 5 to 10 inches of rainfall fell across the northern half of western Washington. The snow level rose to 8000 to 10,000 feet, resulting in some snow melt contributing to runoff.  Widespread minor or greater flooding occurred. Major flooding occurred on the Bogachiel, the three forecast points on the Nooksack, Skagit, and Cowlitz Rivers. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and snow showers occurred overnight, with clearing skies and temperatures dipping to or below freezing in the morning. A vehicle, carrying high school students on their way to school, slid and crashed into a fence and a house around 8am in the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours, a strong cold front passage across the Pendleton forecast area produced strong wind gusts across lower elevation areas, causing reports of damage in north central Oregon and in the Lower Columbia Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hanford Meteorological monitoring network (HMMN) mesonet station HARMY 13.5 miles NW of West Richland, WA measured a peak wind gust of 74 mph at 1515 PST. A broadcast media partner in the Tri-Cities reported that strong winds through the area was responsible for a partial topple of a large building under construction around 1530 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive rain in the northern Cascade Mountains and southern British Columbia led to rising rivers in northern Washington in mid-November. The Okanogan and Similkameen Rivers reached minor flood stage. Most impacts of the flooding rivers were on flood plain and pasture lands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Similkameen River, near Nighthawk and Oroville, reached minor flood stage on November 16th. There were post-flood surveys completed that found several properties damaged due to flooding. The flood impacted pasture land, structures, and crops both upstream and downstream of the Similkameen River gage at Nighthawk. The property and crop damage is an estimate due to no official reports of private and public land damage cost. The Similkameen River gage, near Nighthawk, crested at 14.41' on November 16th at 15:00 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough coupled with surface cold front passing over the region from Dec. 22nd through early Dec. 24th brought moderate snow showers across mountain areas in Northeast Oregon and Southeast Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Easton, WA estimated 36 inches of snow accumulated in a 2 to 3 day period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level weather system pushed a cold front into the Pacific Northwest late Dec. 18th, where the frontal boundary had stalled across southeast Washington and northeast Oregon through Dec. 20th where the stationary frontal boundary had weakened in the afternoon. As a result, bands of rain, freezing rain, and snow were produced across portions of the forecast area during the 19th and 20th, with snow being reported across the lower elevations in the Columbia Basin and mountain locations across southeast Washington and northeast Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Ski Bluewood, WA, 12 inches of new snow was measured in a 36 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 5 to 10 inches caused flooding on many rivers, including the Bogachiel, Nooksack, Samish, Skagit, Skokomish, Stillaguamish Rivers. Crop damages are unknown. Much of the reported damage amounts were for the episodes in November and some of the amounts for this episode were an estimated proportion for the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and some snow melt caused flooding throughout much of Skagit County, especially on the Skagit and Samish Rivers. Flooding on the Skagit started on the 12th, then temporarily dropped below flood stage, before rising to major flood on the night of the 14th. 2 fatalities occurred during this time with many road, highways, and bridges  closed due to water over roadway, washouts, sloughing, and debris. Town inundated including Rockport, Hamilton, Lyman, Sedro Woolley, and Mount Vernon.  There was urban and small stream flooding as well on the 15th.The heavy rain also triggered a number of landslides throughout the area. Damages are reported and estimated; not all damages have been identified. Crop damages are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The wind event of Nov 15, 2021 was a ���warm sector��� wind event, which are  characterized by the Inland Northwest being in the warm sector of an atmospheric river event for multiple days and lead to high winds. Most damage to the region were trees down, power lines down, and roof damage. Avista Utilities and Inland Power reported that approximately 50,000 customers were without power in the height of the storm in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong, gusty winds created widespread damage across Spokane County. With winds ranging from 50-62 mph, the peak gusts 62 mph were reached at the Spokane International Airport in the late afternoon. There was a portion of the metal roof ripped off the Fed-Ex building at the airport. There were numerous reports of obstructing trees and power lines down to leading widespread outages. The winds led to damage to buildings' siding and large signs across Spokane.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a minor flood on the Okanogan River with a majority of the flood impacts found north of Tonasket. In this area, the flood plain is wide and flood water is able to spread. This includes a lot of pasture land or swampy areas near the river. In Ellisforde, there was a farm that had flooding on the property, which included pasture lands and outbuildings. The Okanogan River gage near Tonasket, at minor flood stage, crested at 15.48' at 12:00 PST on November 17th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and some snow melt caused flooding that exceeded major severity on the South Fork Nooksack River and mainstem Nooksack River at North Cedarville and Ferndale. There was urban and small stream flooding as well, hitting Bellingham especially hard. The heavy rain also triggered a dozen landslides throughout the area. Significant flooding on the Nooksack River also overflowed at the City of Everson to the degree that flood waters flowed north along an old Nooksack floodway and Johnson Creek where it flooded the cities of Everson and Nooksack, WA, then combined with flooding from the Sumas River and flooded the City of Sumas, WA where over 85% of homes sustained water damage. Damages are reported and estimated; not all damages have been identified. Crop damages are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave disturbance brought moderate to heavy snow, with strong winds, to the Cascade Mountains on November 9th. The heaviest snow occurred at the Cascade crest and mountain passes. The first round of snow arrived with the front in the morning with periods of heavy snow over Stevens Pass picking back up in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington State Department of Transportation measured 11 of new snow at Stevens Pass on November 9th. The new snow accumulation grew to a total of 37. With heavy snow, there were gusty winds up to 30-40 mph in morning. These winds created blowing snow and poor visibility at the mountain pass on U.S. Highway 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the Inland Northwest in the afternoon and evening of August 20th. The northern counties received the greatest rainfall totals where stationary thunderstorms brought heavy downpours to the area that evening. There were numerous 12 hour observations of 1 to 2 rain reported across Okanogan, Ferry, and Stevens Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall, with reports of 0.40 - 0.50 in one hour, occurred in the early evening hours of August 20th outside the town of Conconully. The brief heavy rainfall lead to 4 to 6 inches of debris flow on the roadway. Some local observation sites reported a total 0.75 - 1.25 of rainfall after a long, evening of showers. Additionally due to the heavy rain, a roadway in Twisp was covered in mud and gravel. There were CoCoRaHS and spotter reports of over 2.0 of rain in Twisp that evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the Inland Northwest in the afternoon and evening of August 20th. The northern counties received the greatest rainfall totals where stationary thunderstorms brought heavy downpours to the area that evening. There were numerous 12 hour observations of 1 to 2 rain reported across Okanogan, Ferry, and Stevens Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a rock slide, partially blocking a road near a golf course in Orondo, due to brief, heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Passing scattered showers on the evening of July 12th, with embedded lightning, ignited several wildfires as they passed across eastern Washington. The summer season of hot and dry conditions fueled fires across the Colville Reservation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited the Chuweah Creek Wildfire on the Colville Indian Reservation, just outside of Nespelem, on July 12th. This was signficantly driven by strong winds and very dry conditions. There are no known injuries or fatalities. Damages include 7 buildings/homes, 7 outbuildings, and numerous cars. The quick spreading fire did not give all farmers time to evacuate livestock which lead to many horse and cattle injuries and fatalities. The Chuweah Creek Fire burned a total 36,752 acres before it was contained on August 30th. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $8,915,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge of high pressure began to build into the region around August 11, remaining in place through August 13 before pushing eastward. As the upper level ridge influence increased over the region, the lower level flow turned easterly, creating a downsloping effect across much of Western Washington. As a result of this setup, very warm temperatures were forecast for most of the area, with 850mb temperatures expected to peak near 25C during the heart of the event, with surface temperatures ranging from the mid 80s to near 100 and low temperatures during the overnight period ranging from the mid 60s to low 70s.||Excessive Heat Watch was issued on August 8, several days prior to the event, valid for all included zones beginning 12PM on Wednesday August 11 and continuing for some zones through Friday August 13 at 8PM and others Saturday August 14 at 7PM. The Watch was upgraded to a warning during the afternoon of August 10.||Fire weather was also a concern during the event, along with air quality impacts from smoke, however, no major impacts were noted during the period of unusually warm weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of much above normal temperatures affected the Seattle area around mid-August. High temperatures ranged from the mid 80s to low 90s during this period, with the temperature peaking August 12-August 13 at 93 degrees, both of which were record high temperatures. Low temperatures during this period ranged from the mid to upper 60s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge of high pressure began to build into the region around August 11, remaining in place through August 13 before pushing eastward. As the upper level ridge influence increased over the region, the lower level flow turned easterly, creating a downsloping effect across much of Western Washington. As a result of this setup, very warm temperatures were forecast for most of the area, with 850mb temperatures expected to peak near 25C during the heart of the event, with surface temperatures ranging from the mid 80s to near 100 and low temperatures during the overnight period ranging from the mid 60s to low 70s.||Excessive Heat Watch was issued on August 8, several days prior to the event, valid for all included zones beginning 12PM on Wednesday August 11 and continuing for some zones through Friday August 13 at 8PM and others Saturday August 14 at 7PM. The Watch was upgraded to a warning during the afternoon of August 10.||Fire weather was also a concern during the event, along with air quality impacts from smoke, however, no major impacts were noted during the period of unusually warm weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southwest Interior experienced a stretch of heat towards mid August. The ASOS sensor at Olympia showed high temperatures in the low to mid 90s through the duration of the heat event, with a record high temperatures of 96 recorded on August 12. Low temperatures through the event ranged from the mid to upper 50s.||Centralia also recorded temperatures in the low to mid 90s. A high temperature of 95 was recorded on August 12 and August 13. Low temperatures stayed in the mid 60s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight and early morning scattered showers on July 7th, with embedded lightning, ignited several wildfires as they passed across the region. Weeks of hot and dry conditions fueled fires across southeastern Washington throughout August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited several wildfires across Garfield and Asotin counties on July 7th: Dry Gulch, Lick Creek, and Silcott. The Silcott Fire was contained in four days after burning 8,633 acres. By the 8th, Dry Gulch and Lick Creek fires merged and were fought as Lick Creek Fire until containment on August 26th. Steep slopes with insect infestation and dead material from past fires hindered access to fight the fire. The hot and dry conditions added to the vast acreage burned. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the 80,638 acre wildfire was calculated at $24,379,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active weather pattern set up in October, bringing multiple rounds of wind and precipitation to both the lowlands and mountains of western Washington. A particularly stronger system in late October was responsible for stronger winds, heavier precipitation, and high surf. A High Wind Warning was in effect for the Coast, Western Strait of Juan de Fuca, and the Northern Interior locations, with a Wind Advisory for much of the Puget Sound vicinity and Eastern Strait of Juan de Fuca.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports on social media of trees down in the Redmond and Bellevue vicinity, making for road detours or road closures. In Redmond, KC Road services reported overhead wires down on 204th Place N.E. near SR 202 around 1500L, causing the road to be closed until wires & trees are safely removed. In Bellevue, a social media posts reported at Sammamish HS at 9:10am- the wind knocked down a big branch which took down a power line, obstructing the side road. Another social media post indicated a tree fallen on a house in Issaquah, with no major damage and no reported injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of Monday September 27, 2021 showers in the area were quite active as a cool upper trough moved into the region. One storm cell produced an EF-0 tornado in Battle Ground, WA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado with estimated winds of 85 mph (DI:28 ,DOD:3) began near the intersection of NE 72nd Avenue and State Highway 502 at 45.7804 latitude, -122.5982 longitude. The tornado tracked in a northeast direction, ending up near NE 259th St. and NE 147th Ave at 45.8105 latitude and -122.5229 longitude.  The tornado path was about 240 yards wide with a track length of about 3.9 miles. The estimated start and end time of the tornado is 1806PST and 1821PST.  No injuries or fatalities were reported. The majority of the damage was to trees that were uprooted or snapped. Other damage included a tree that fell on an RV, and fences damaged by toppled trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep low pressure system off the Pacific NW coast pushed a strong front across northwest Oregon and southwest Washington, resulting in strong winds and scattered thunderstorms. One severe thunderstorm developed in the afternoon across northern Clark County. This storm produced wind damage from Woodland, WA to near Lake Merwin.    Wind knocked out power to over 5000 residents in Clark County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm Survey for convective wind damage around 1 pm at Lake Merwin, WA. Somewhat significant tree damage at the Woodland Park area, as well as a fresh large tree, roughly 2 feet, snapped near the Lewis River RV park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There is considerable damage to the Okanogan-Wenatchee and Colville National Forests due to three long-living wildfires: Nason, TwentyFive Mile, and Walker Creek. They burned over 50,000 acres, which were mostly uninhabited areas. One of the three fires was ignited by lightning storms and all were fueled by many days of red flag conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited the Nason Fire, seven miles west of Lake Wenatchee, on August 4th. The fire was located in steep, rocky terrain that made it difficult for firefighters to safely access. The fire was slow with limited growth due to the shaded and moist, sub-alpine environment. The fire burned a total of 1,269 acres of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forecast before fully contained on October 19th. There are no known injuries or structures lost. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $300,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There is considerable damage to the Okanogan-Wenatchee and Colville National Forests due to three long-living wildfires: Nason, TwentyFive Mile, and Walker Creek. They burned over 50,000 acres, which were mostly uninhabited areas. One of the three fires was ignited by lightning storms and all were fueled by many days of red flag conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ignited due to an undetermined source on August 3rd, the Walker Creek Fire surrounded Bonaparte Lake and Wacounda in the Colville National Forest. On August 14th, it merged with the Spur Fire and from then on, they were managed as the Walker Creek Fire. Due to the proximity of communities, there were numerous level 3 evacuations for the first month. Three single residences and 14 minor structures were damaged or destroyed. Shorter burn windows, with cool and showery weather, led to the slow containment of the Walker Creek Fire. It burned a total of 23,733 acres before fully contained on October 15th. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fires was calculated at $26,171,346.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN STRAIT OF JUAN DE FUCA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal system passed across Western Washington in the middle of September, bringing heavy rain, high elevation snowfall, gusty winds, and a few thunderstorms to the area. This was the first notable frontal system in several months, prompting the issuance of several weather headlines. The active weather was most pronounced across the area Friday 9/17 through Saturday 9/18, with wind of highest impact, particularly the Admiralty Inlet area, Jefferson County, and Western Skagit County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds affected the Eastern Strait of Juan de Fuca for much of the day on Friday 9/17. Downed trees were reported across much of Jefferson County, eventually prompting an upgrade from a Wind Advisory to a High Warning Warning. The following reports were gathered:||-Tree limbs were reported to be downed onto wires at Hastings and Howard in Port Townsend.||-The WSDOT ferry route from Port Townsend/Coupeville service was suspended due to strong winds and rough seas.||-A low hanging power line had US 101 at MP 276 near Old Gardiner Road reduced to one lane with alternating traffic. Old Gardiner Road was also blocked.||-Trees down on US 101 both directions at MP 167.59 near Lower Hoh Rd beginning at 1:16 pm on Sept. 17. Traffic was alternated through the area, with the area cleared an hour later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There is considerable damage to the Okanogan-Wenatchee and Colville National Forests due to three long-living wildfires: Nason, TwentyFive Mile, and Walker Creek. They burned over 50,000 acres, which were mostly uninhabited areas. One of the three fires was ignited by lightning storms and all were fueled by many days of red flag conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Twentyfive Mile Fire, west of Lake Chelan, was ignited on August 15th with the cause of the fire remains under investigation. Cooler and wetter weather, in mid-September, helped slow fire behavior, but return to drier and warmer weather fueled fire activity. The proximity to Lake Chelan were useful for frequent water drops in rugged terrain. The fire burned a total of 22,117 acres of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forecast and Twentyfive Mile Creek State Park before fully contained on October 19th. There were two homes destroyed with no known injuries or fatalities. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $31,887,321.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As cold air was deep and entrenched along the east slopes of the Cascades, passing precipitation systems brought heavy precipitation to the Cascades. Total snow accumulations, from December 22 through 24th, were between 15 to 30 inches in locations such as Stevens Pass, Holden Village, and Stehekin. Winds reached gusts of 25 mph and created blowing snow in the Cascade gaps.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow with strong gusty winds led to poor visibility and dangerous driving conditions in the Cascades. The Washington Department of Transportation reported that Highway 2, at Stevens Pass, was closed due to multiple collisions and spin outs on December 23rd. Stevens Pass measured a three day total snow accumulation of 32 inches. Due to the short term, heavy snowfall, avalanche control was performed following the winter storm's passage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered snow showers tracked across the region as low pressure edged inland and disturbances round it. There were some intense snow showers across portions of the Palouse to the Spokane - Coeur d'Alene area and accompanied by strong, gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy, blowing snow led to white out conditions throughout Spokane Valley. The sudden snow squall and hazardous driving conditions led to stranded cars and multiple accidents, especially along Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure over the Northeast Pacific Ocean put the region into a strong northerly flow and allowed for an intrusion of cold, dry Arctic air. The low temperatures averaged about 15 to 30 degrees below normal for the last week of the year. The accompanying breezy winds on December 27th brought wind chills into the single digits to below zero lows. The city of Spokane opened a temporary drop in shelter for homeless during the extreme cold temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Washington Department of Health reported that there was 1 cold-related death on December 27th in Spokane County. The exact time of death is unknown. Minimum wind chills were in the single digits across the county including 4 degrees at Spokane International Airport and 7 degrees at Felts Field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 600dm ridge of high pressure centered over the Desert Southwest in mid-June retrograded over to the West Coast/East-Central Pacific Ocean by the start of the 3rd week of June. The pattern began to amplify on June 20th, with the ridge axis building along the West Coast, coinciding with a large area of low pressure deepening over Hudson Bay to the east. This feature, along with deepening low pressure over the Gulf of Alaska were the primary factors in the omega block setting up off the Pacific Northwest Coast by the end of the week. The strength of the Hudson Bay vortex kept the 600dm ridge high centered over the Pacific Northwest for nearly a week, yielding record breaking temperatures (multiple days of over 100 degree temperatures in a row with low temperatures resembling the average high temperatures for the time of year).||The record-breaking heat from this anomalous pattern led to multiple heat-related fatalities (heat stroke and heat stress) across Western Washington is also expected to be related to numerous drownings across the area, largely in part due to hypothermia as people sought reprieve from the heat in local area lakes and large bodies of water. In addition, several brush fires were also ignited due to dry fuels present amidst hot temperatures. All fires were contained without major impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A section of pavement came loose/buckled on NB I-405 and I-5 in Tukwila, causing road and highway closures for several hours during the evening commute.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong Pacific Storm system brought heavy snow to the mainly the North and Central Cascades. The frontal system also brought breezy winds to the Western Washington lowlands, but no notable impacts and/or high wind were verified.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall around midnight on the 18th, with a quick 15 inches of new snow reported at the Mount Baker Ski Resort in a 12 hour period by noon (on the 18th). Heavy snow then continued into the 19th, with 21 inches reported in a 24 hours period at the same location previously mentioned. Snow then quickly tapered during the early morning hours of the 19th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another strong Pacific Storm system brought heavy snow to the mainly the North and Central Cascades. The frontal system also brought breezy winds to the Western Washington lowlands, but no notable impacts and/or high wind were verified.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began around midnight on December 18th, with a few inches of new snow accumulation by daybreak (on the 18th). Snow then quickly became heavier, with over a foot reported in a 12 hour period from 6am on the 18th to 6pm on the 18th. Snow then quickly tapered into the 19th. Snoqualmie Pass also received new snowfall, but snowfall amounts were not heavy enough to reach winter storm criteria.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF WHATCOM AND SKAGIT COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems amidst a much colder airmass over the Pacific Northwest yielded a long duration mountain snow event, affecting both the Cascades and the Olympics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow began on December 22 during the late evening hours, with 18 inches of new snow reported at the Mount Baker Ski Resort in a 24 hour period ending during the late evening hours of the 23rd. Heavy snow fell through much of the 23rd, with total snowfall pushing the 25-30 inch range by the 24th. Snow tapered for a short period of time on the 24th but quickly ramped back up on the 25th. Snow then continued through the 26th before tapering very late in the day. New snow depth reports at the Ski Resort were around 40 inches for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate snow accumulation moved across eastern Washington in the afternoon on December 16th, ranging from 1 to 3 inches. Air temperatures were in the upper 20s and lower 30s. Road surface temperatures were between 32 - 34 degrees before sunset when they dropped below freezing. This created a mixture of compact snow and ice on roadways as melting snow re-froze and mixed with new snow. This impacted major interstates, highways, arterials and secondary roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slippery driving conditions, created by refreezing precipitation, led to numerous accidents during the evening commute in Spokane metropolitan area. The icy roads caused multiple vehicle pile ups, slide-offs, and road blockages. The Spokane Police Department issued an emergency alert requesting residents limit their driving due to the hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving shortwave, followed by a surface low, brought two rounds of snow showers from December 24th through December 26th. There were multiple bands of moderate to localized heavy snow across northeastern Washington. There was some quick snow accumulation rates ranged from 0.5 to 2 inches in a couple of hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A multi-day winter storm moved through northeastern Washington from December 24th into early 26th. Over a 24 hour time period, it brought heavy snow with 5 to 10 inches of snow accumulating across areas such as Metaline Falls, Deer Park, and Newport. Early on the 25th, the 49 Degrees North Ski Resort reported 15 to 20 inches inches of snow in the past 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system brought snow into the Cascades on December 10th with snow becoming heavy overnight into late December 11th. Travel became very difficult over mountain passes and roadways due to the blowing snow from accompanying strong, gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved across the Cascades late December 10th through the 11th. There were 10 to 14 inches of snow accumulating over 24 hours in locations such as Lucerne and Stehekin. Stevens Pass reported a two day total of 30.5 inches of snow. There were gusty winds with gusts up to 40 mph reported across the Cascade ridge tops. Travel became very difficult on US Highway 2, especially across Stevens Pass, due to heavy, blowing snow. These poor travel conditions lead to numerous slide-offs and accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system brought snow into the Cascades on December 10th with snow becoming heavy overnight into late December 11th. Travel became very difficult over mountain passes and roadways due to the blowing snow from accompanying strong, gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved through eastern Washington late December 10th into the 11th. It brought heavy snow, with 6 to 8 inches of snow accumulating, in the eastern Cascade valleys, including the towns of Plain and Leavenworth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system brought snow into the Cascades on December 10th with snow becoming heavy overnight into late December 11th. Travel became very difficult over mountain passes and roadways due to the blowing snow from accompanying strong, gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved through eastern Washington late December 10th into the 11th. It brought heavy snow, with 4 to 8 inches of snow accumulating, in the northeastern Cascade valleys, including the towns of Mazama and Winthrop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 600dm ridge of high pressure centered over the Desert Southwest in mid-June retrograded over to the West Coast/East-Central Pacific Ocean by the start of the 3rd week of June. The pattern began to amplify on June 20th, with the ridge axis building along the West Coast, coinciding with a large area of low pressure deepening over Hudson Bay to the east. This feature, along with deepening low pressure over the Gulf of Alaska were the primary factors in the omega block setting up off the Pacific Northwest Coast by the end of the week. The strength of the Hudson Bay vortex kept the 600dm ridge high centered over the Pacific Northwest for nearly a week, yielding record breaking temperatures (multiple days of over 100 degree temperatures in a row with low temperatures resembling the average high temperatures for the time of year).||The record-breaking heat from this anomalous pattern led to multiple heat-related fatalities (heat stroke and heat stress) across Western Washington is also expected to be related to numerous drownings across the area, largely in part due to hypothermia as people sought reprieve from the heat in local area lakes and large bodies of water. In addition, several brush fires were also ignited due to dry fuels present amidst hot temperatures. All fires were contained without major impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State route 544 milepost 7 near Everson was closed as asphalt roadway buckled and was deemed unsafe for travel. Detours were put in place with highway repair complete the morning following the incident.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Concrete panels along NB I-5 to I-705 experienced period damage and needed continuous repairs from Jun 27-Jun 29. This caused 3 lanes of the collector-distributor to close, causing traffic backups and detours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems amidst a much colder airmass over the Pacific Northwest yielded a long duration mountain snow event, affecting both the Cascades and the Olympics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snowfall spanned across of several day period across the Olympics from Dec 22-Dec 26. Heavy snow began during the late evening hours on Dec 22nd and accumulated rapidly (according to reports from Hurricane Ridge) through Dec 23rd. Snow then finally tapered on Dec 26th.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF SNOHOMISH AND KING COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems amidst a much colder airmass over the Pacific Northwest yielded a long duration mountain snow event, affecting both the Cascades and the Olympics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall in the Central Cascades during the late evening hours of Dec 22, with several inches of new snow reported at both Stevens and Snoqualmie Pass, but it wasn't until 24th when much heavier snow began. On December 24, 12 inches of new snow was received in a 12 hour period at Stevens Pass. Steady snow then continued through the 26th before then tapering off.||Although Snoqualmie Pass received a prolonged period of snowfall during these events, there was not a specific period of time where snowfall amounts met or exceeded winter storm criteria. For this prolonged event, Stevens Pass received more snowfall than Snoqualmie Pass.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration period of heavy snow affected the Southern Cascades during the latter part of December. Snow began on Dec 22 and really ramped up in intensity around Dec 24-25, with about 2 feet of new snow reported after the Christmas holiday.

CZ_NAME: CASCADES OF PIERCE AND LEWIS COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active weather across Western Washington continued through the end of the month, with another storm system bringing a quick period of heavy snowfall to the Southern Cascades, including Mount Rainier National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick period of heavy snow resulted in 12-13 inches of new snow at Paradise at Mount Rainier in a 12 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter weather system, dropping in from the northwest, brought bands of snow across eastern Washington throughout the day, December 6th. Following the cold front passage, temperatures dropped well below freezing overnight leading to icing on roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Early morning light snow across Spokane County led to hazardous driving conditions throughout the day. The Spokane Police Department received reports of over 100 collisions due to slick roadways. Overnight, roads iced over and led to multiple collisions and rollovers the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous storm system produced locally heavy snow over the Cascade crest at Stevens Pass. The snow showers continued throughout the day into early the next day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to accumulate at 0700PST December 8th and tapered off mid-day December 9th. A total of 20 inches of snow fell in 24 hours on US Highway 2 at Stevens Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter weather system brought moderate to heavy snow over the Palouse into Spokane, Lewiston-Clarkston Valley, and Blue Mountains. The snow came in two waves: first wave in the morning with light snow resulting in slippery roads for the morning commute and the second in the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to accumulate around 0000 PST and tapered off by 1600 PST. A total of 4 to 6 inches of snow fell from across the Washington Palouse, including Pullman and Colfax. There were reports of drifting snow and poor driving conditions across U.S. Highway 195 and State Route 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter weather system brought moderate to heavy snow over the Palouse into Spokane, Lewiston-Clarkston Valley, and Blue Mountains. The snow came in two waves: first wave in the morning with light snow resulting in slippery roads for the morning commute and the second in the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of snow accumulations reaching totals of 9 inches by the late afternoon in Clarkston. The event time fame is approximate and Lewiston Airport was used for reference.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dome of hot high pressure over the Inland Northwest pushed afternoon high temperatures into record territory. In late June 2021, a long-lived heatwave broke many daily, monthly, and all time maximum high and low temperature records at several stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of excessive heat occurred across the Wenatchee area from June 26th to July 2nd. Afternoon high temperatures ranged from 102 to 114 degrees most afternoons. Overnight lows only fell into the mid-to-upper 70 degrees. There were two all time records set in the Wenatchee area. On the 28th, a new record high maximum temperature was set at Chelan with a high of 111 degrees. On the 30th, Wenatchee's overnight low only dropped to 79 degrees setting a new record high minimum temperature. Numerous cooling centers opened for those without air conditioning during the extreme heat. There are no known heat related deaths.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dome of hot high pressure over the Inland Northwest pushed afternoon high temperatures into record territory. In late June 2021, a long-lived heatwave broke many daily, monthly, and all time maximum high and low temperature records at several stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two new all time records were set across the Palouse. The town of Rosalia set a new all-time maximum temperature of 111 degrees on June 28th and Pullman reached 106 degrees on June 29th. There was a report, from the Washington State Department of Transportation, that there was pavement buckling on Highway 195 due to the heat. Due to the extreme high temperatures, Pullman cooling center was established for those without air conditioning across the area. There are no known heat related deaths.

The pattern began to amplify on June 20th, with the ridge axis building along the West Coast, coinciding with a large area of low pressure deepening over Hudson Bay to the east. This feature, along with deepening low pressure over the Gulf of Alaska were the primary factors in the omega block setting up off the Pacific Northwest Coast by the end of the week. The strength of the Hudson Bay vortex kept the 600dm ridge high centered over the Pacific Northwest for nearly a week, yielding record breaking temperatures (multiple days of over 100 degree temperatures in a row with low temperatures resembling the average high temperatures for the time of year).||The record-breaking heat from this anomalous pattern led to multiple heat-related fatalities (heat stroke and heat stress) across Western Washington is also expected to be related to numerous drownings across the area, largely in part due to hypothermia as people sought reprieve from the heat in local area lakes and large bodies of water. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The left two lanes of I-90 eastbound five miles east of North Bend closed due to the highway buckling from the heat. This lead to traffic and difficult travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight severe thunderstorms brought rain showers and lightning through eastern Washington on June 15th. There were heavy downpours and isolated hail in portions of southeastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of hail, from 1 to 1.25 inches, fell in Pomeroy as two strong thunderstorm cells moved across northern Garfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overnight severe thunderstorms brought rain showers and lightning through eastern Washington on June 15th. There were heavy downpours and isolated hail in portions of southeastern Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of hail, from 1 to 1.5 inches, associated with two strong thunderstorm cells that moved across western Whitman County. The reports were from Endicott, Peyton, St. John, and La Crosse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter weather system brought moderate to heavy snow over the Palouse into Spokane, Lewiston-Clarkston Valley, and Blue Mountains. The snow came in two waves: first wave in the morning with light snow resulting in slippery roads for the morning commute and the second in the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating snow overnight led to on-ramps and off-ramps at the exits packed with snow and ice in the early morning commute. Around 0830 PST, eastbound Interstate 90 in Spokane was fully blocked and closed due to a jackknifed semi-truck. There was a car wedged under the trailer leading to non-life threatening injuries for the driver. There were numerous accidents in the Spokane area due to hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong dome of hot high pressure over the Inland Northwest pushed afternoon high temperatures into record territory. In late June 2021, a long-lived heatwave broke many daily, monthly, and all time maximum high and low temperature records at several stations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From June 26 to 30th, Moses Lake and Ephrata had high temperatures reach between 103 to 116 degrees, which is abnormally warm for end of June. Ephrata broke their all time high maximum temperature when the airport reached 116 degrees on June 29th. Local libraries were arranged to act as cooling centers for residents without air conditioning. There are no know heat-related deaths.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record breaking heat across the Seattle metro area led to multiple heat related fatalities, numerous pavement buckles requiring road closures and repair, the closing of University bridge for marine traffic due to excessive heat, lowering of speed limits to Sound Transit, millions of gallons of water used, the highest energy usage in Seattle public utilities history during the summer months, the closing of the Seattle monorail, and the death of dozens of Caspian Terns.||Temperatures began to rise in the Seattle metro on 6/26 to 102 degrees, followed by an increase in several degrees to 104 on 6/27, with a peak and all time record high temperature on 6/28  of 108. Temperatures slowly began to cool as the month concluded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Record breaking heat affected the Tacoma area for several days, beginning on 6/26 and peaking 6/27-6/28.  A high temperature of 99 was recorded on 6/27, with an (unofficial) all time record high temperature of 105 of 6/27. Temperatures then reached 99 and 97 on the afternoon of 6/28 and 6/29, respectively before a gradual cool down began at the conclusion of the month. Several reports of road buckling/road damage around Tacoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold airmass pushed into Western Washington at the end of December, resulting in cold air amidst sufficient moisture and lift as a modified arctic frontal boundary slid south. This made for significant lowland and mountain snow, with a period of drier but much colder weather for the following days. Several climate sites reached record low temperatures during and shortly after the winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A record low was reported at the Hoquiam ASOS on the morning of Dec 28th at 21F. This replaces the previous record low temperature of 25F from 1978.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 600dm ridge of high pressure centered over the Desert Southwest in mid-June retrograded over to the West Coast/East-Central Pacific Ocean by the start of the 3rd week of June. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A much cooler pattern began to setup over the Pacific Northwest towards the end of December. As Fraser Outflow began to push into Western Whatcom County, it created favorable conditions for freezing rain as cold surface air (several degrees below freezing) settled in with warmer air just above. Winds also became breezy out of the NE, but no major impacts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shortly before 5 PM PST, in the heart of the evening commute, freezing rain began to fall across portions of Western Whatcom County as cold surface air from Fraser Outflow began to filter in below slightly warmer, Pacific-influenced air aloft. State troopers responded to a fatality collision involving multiple vehicles along southbound I-5 at MP 263. Roadway was fully blocked for a period of time. ||A report of around 0.05 inches of freezing rain was reported in Lynden the evening of Dec 21 with a surface temperature ranging from 28-30F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active 2021 wildfire season continued with nine destructive fires burning almost 100,000 acres, contained by the end of September. They were fueled by many days with windy conditions and low relative humidity. Cooler temperatures, with more moisture and wetting rains, helped with containment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Summit Trail Fire was ignited during a lightning storm on July 12th and jointly managed with Upper Lime Creek Fire. Steep, rugged, and often hard to access terrain challenged containment. Additionally, many red flag conditions, with wind ignited spot fires, fueled fire spread. The fire burned a total of 51,328 acres on Colville Reservation before fully contained on September 30th. There are no reports of injuries or structures lost. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $58,000,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active 2021 wildfire season continued with nine destructive fires burning almost 100,000 acres, contained by the end of September. They were fueled by many days with windy conditions and low relative humidity. Cooler temperatures, with more moisture and wetting rains, helped with containment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bulldog Mountain and Mack Mountain fires were jointly managed after ignited approximately five miles apart on August 5th. The cause of the wildfires was lightning after thunderstorms passed through northeastern Washington. Steep, rugged terrain, with limited access, and heavy fuels led to difficulty with containment. There are no reports of injuries or fatalities. The pair of wildfires burned a total 6,723 acres on the Colville National Forest before fully contained on September 30th. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fires was calculated at $5,800,600.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active 2021 wildfire season continued with nine destructive fires burning almost 100,000 acres, contained by the end of September. They were fueled by many days with windy conditions and low relative humidity. Cooler temperatures, with more moisture and wetting rains, helped with containment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the night of August 3rd, passing thunderstorms resulted in several new fire starts on the Colville Indian Reservation, which included the Hamilton and Whitmore Fires, northwest of Nespelem. Gusty, erratic winds effected fire behavior and lead to numerous evacuations of residences and animals. All evacuations were lifted by August 18th, but due to the dry fuels and red flag conditions, the smoldering continued into September. There are no reports of injuries or fatalities. The pair of wildfires burned a total 57,860 acres before fully contained by September 30th. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fires was calculated at $9,320,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active 2021 wildfire season continued with nine destructive fires burning almost 100,000 acres, contained by the end of September. They were fueled by many days with windy conditions and low relative humidity. Cooler temperatures, with more moisture and wetting rains, helped with containment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chickadee Creek Fire was ignited by an unknown source on August 4th and jointly managed with Walker Fire. Fire fighting crews faced many set backs such as red flag conditions, spot fires, and inclement weather preventing infrared aircraft flights. The fire burned a total of 5,855 acres of timber before fully contained on September 17th. There are no known injuries or structures lost. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $5,800,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four wildfires, ignited between July 7th and 16th, burned over 120,000 acres of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest and public land in Okanogan County. These included the Cedar Creek, Varden, Cub Creek 2, and Delancy fires. All fires were fueled by many days of red flag conditions. Increased humidity, cooler temperatures, and precipitation helped with slow containment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar Creek, Delancy, and Varden wildfires were ignited by lightning during passing widespread thunderstorms, from July 8 to 11th, near Mazama. There was minimal activity by the Delancy Fire, which grew to 225 acres. Varden Fire grew 700 acres before merging with, and from then on referred to as, Cedar Creek Fire. This pair of fires burned a total 56,572 acres of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest with containment by the end of September. There was very steep and rocky terrain, which made fire fighting efforts difficult. There are no known injuries/fatalities or structures damaged. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the three fires was calculated at a total of $41,562,323.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four wildfires, ignited between July 7th and 16th, burned over 120,000 acres of the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest and public land in Okanogan County. These included the Cedar Creek, Varden, Cub Creek 2, and Delancy fires. All fires were fueled by many days of red flag conditions. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cub Creek 2 Wildfire was human caused, due to a spark from illegal activity, on July 16th. The fire was ignited five miles north of Mazama and grew to a total 70,186 acres before contained by the end of September. Several areas within the Cub Creek 2 Wildfire remained closed, after containment, due to public safety concerns such as tree hazards or increased probability of debris flows. There are no known injuries/fatalities or structures destroyed. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $27,788,529.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited the Muckamuck Wildfire, which spread in and around the town of Conconully, on August 4th. The fire was on lands managed by Colville National Forest that had not burned in several years. There are no known injuries or fatalities. There were seven structures lost: four primary and three secondary. The fire burned a total of 13,297 acres before fully contained on September 16th. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $14,009,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active 2021 wildfire season continued with nine destructive fires burning almost 100,000 acres, contained by the end of September. They were fueled by many days with windy conditions and low relative humidity. Cooler temperatures, with more moisture and wetting rains, helped with containment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ford Corkscrew Wildfire was ignited, near the town of Ford, due to an unknown cause on August 15th. The fire spread to the east, further threatening the towns and communities of Tum Tum, Springdale, Clayton, and Loon Lake, which led to numerous evacuations. Ford Corkscrew Fire destroyed at least 18 residences and eight outhouses or barns. There are no reports of injuries or fatalities. The fire burned a total of 15,777 acres before fully contained on September 17th. The cost of the firefighting resources required to contain the fire was calculated at $8,000,000.
